Diabetes Risk Prediction — Exploratory Analysis and Machine Learning

Vicente Valero

2026-03-25

1 Overview

This project analyses a publicly available clinical diabetes dataset from hbiostat.org to build and evaluate binary classification models for diabetes risk prediction. The dataset contains clinical, biochemical, and anthropometric measurements from 403 patients.

The central question is whether a combination of routinely available parameters — blood glucose, lipid profile, BMI, blood pressure, and waist-to-hip ratio — can reliably identify individuals at high risk of type 2 diabetes, with a specific focus on models applicable in community pharmacy settings.

2 1 — Data Loading and Initial Inspection

## First records:
## 
## Dataset structure:
## 'data.frame':    403 obs. of  19 variables:
##  $ id      : int  1000 1001 1002 1003 1005 1008 1011 1015 1016 1022 ...
##  $ chol    : int  203 165 228 78 249 248 195 227 177 263 ...
##  $ stab.glu: int  82 97 92 93 90 94 92 75 87 89 ...
##  $ hdl     : int  56 24 37 12 28 69 41 44 49 40 ...
##  $ ratio   : num  3.6 6.9 6.2 6.5 8.9 ...
##  $ glyhb   : num  4.31 4.44 4.64 4.63 7.72 ...
##  $ location: chr  "Buckingham" "Buckingham" "Buckingham" "Buckingham" ...
##  $ age     : int  46 29 58 67 64 34 30 37 45 55 ...
##  $ gender  : chr  "female" "female" "female" "male" ...
##  $ height  : int  62 64 61 67 68 71 69 59 69 63 ...
##  $ weight  : int  121 218 256 119 183 190 191 170 166 202 ...
##  $ frame   : chr  "medium" "large" "large" "large" ...
##  $ bp.1s   : int  118 112 190 110 138 132 161 NA 160 108 ...
##  $ bp.1d   : int  59 68 92 50 80 86 112 NA 80 72 ...
##  $ bp.2s   : int  NA NA 185 NA NA NA 161 NA 128 NA ...
##  $ bp.2d   : int  NA NA 92 NA NA NA 112 NA 86 NA ...
##  $ waist   : int  29 46 49 33 44 36 46 34 34 45 ...
##  $ hip     : int  38 48 57 38 41 42 49 39 40 50 ...
##  $ time.ppn: int  720 360 180 480 300 195 720 1020 300 240 ...

The dataset contains 403 patients and 19 variables:

Category Variables
Demographic / ID id, age, gender, location, frame
Biochemical chol, stab.glu, hdl, ratio, glyhb
Anthropometric height, weight, waist, hip
Vital signs bp.1s, bp.1d, bp.2s, bp.2d
Other time.ppn

Missing values are concentrated in duplicate blood pressure measurements and in Bare.nuclei-equivalent fields. The key outcome variable glyhb (glycated haemoglobin) has fewer than 5% missing values, making it a viable basis for defining a binary diabetes label (glyhb ≥ 6.5%).

4 3 — Exploratory Data Analysis

4.1 3.1 — Cholesterol/HDL Ratio vs Age by Glycaemic Status

The cholesterol/HDL ratio distribution is right-skewed, with most values between 3 and 6 but a notable tail of individuals exceeding 10, indicating elevated cardiovascular risk. The trend line shows no strong systematic increase of the ratio with age; metabolic factors such as glycaemic control appear more determinant than chronological age for lipid imbalance in this sample.

4.2 3.2 — Ratio vs Glycated Haemoglobin (glyhb)

There is a moderate positive association between HbA1c and the cholesterol/HDL ratio (R² = 0.208): poorer glycaemic control tends to coincide with a worse lipid profile, reflecting shared metabolic dysregulation pathways between glucose homeostasis and lipid balance.

4.3 3.3 — Ratio by Sex

Females in this sample present a slightly higher median ratio with greater dispersion than males, suggesting potential sex-related differences in lipid profiles.

4.4 3.4 — Cholesterol Level Distribution

Most individuals fall within the normal cholesterol range (<200 mg/dL), but a significant proportion sits in the borderline high band (200–240 mg/dL), representing a relevant target for preventive intervention.

5 4 — Probability Analysis

5.1 4.1 — Cardiovascular Risk Classification

## CARDIOVASCULAR RISK DISTRIBUTION
## 
##      High risk       Low risk  Moderate risk Very high risk 
##             40             38             45             13
## 
## Percentages:
## 
##      High risk       Low risk  Moderate risk Very high risk 
##          29.41          27.94          33.09           9.56

5.2 4.2 — Probability Statements

P(high or very high CV risk):

## P(high or very high CV risk): 38.97 %

P(diabetes | ratio > 5) — conditional probability:

## P(diabetes | ratio > 5): 36.54 %

P(female ∩ moderate CV risk) — joint probability:

## P(female ∩ moderate risk): 22.06 %

5.3 4.3 — Simulation: Normal Approximation of the Ratio Distribution

## OBSERVED vs SIMULATED
## Observed mean: 4.76  | Simulated mean: 4.8
## Observed SD:   2.05  | Simulated SD:   2.03

The normal approximation reproduces the mean and standard deviation faithfully (observed: μ = 4.77, σ = 2.07; simulated: μ ≈ 4.80, σ ≈ 2.05). However, the observed distribution has a heavier right tail: there are more individuals with extreme ratios (> 8–10) than a Gaussian model predicts. This right-skew indicates a subgroup with notably worse lipid profiles that a simple normal simulation underestimates.

6 5 — Machine Learning: Diabetes Classification

Binary outcome: glyhb ≥ 6.5% = diabetes (1), otherwise = no diabetes (0).

6.1 5.1 — Feature Engineering and Train/Test Split

## Variables available: 11 of 11

##  [4] "hdl"               "stab.glu"          "bmi"

## [10] "ratio"             "diabetes_binary"
## 
## Rows before cleaning: 136
## Rows after cleaning:  136
## Outcome distribution:
## 
##   0   1 
## 103  33
## Diabetes prevalence (glyhb ≥ 6.5%): 24.3 %
## Training set: 97 observations
## Test set:     39 observations
## Train/test split: 71.3 / 28.7 %

6.4 5.4 — Model Comparison

Logistic regression offers full interpretability: coefficients translate directly into odds ratios with confidence intervals, supporting evidence-based clinical communication. Its main limitation is the linearity assumption, which may underrepresent complex interactions (e.g. age × obesity, glucose × lipids) common in metabolic disease.

Random Forest captures non-linear patterns and variable interactions without parametric constraints, achieving higher sensitivity (82.4% vs 76.5%) and AUC (0.890 vs 0.870). The trade-off is reduced transparency — the ensemble structure makes causal interpretation difficult, and the model is more prone to overfitting on small samples.

For mass screening in community pharmacy, interpretable models with clear clinical thresholds offer better risk-benefit balance. For research or specialist tools, the predictive power of ensemble methods justifies their complexity. Hybrid approaches — combining the transparency of regression with the flexibility of ensemble methods — represent a promising direction.

7 6 — Interactive Shiny Application

The app below lets users explore the diabetes dataset interactively, selecting X and Y variables for scatter plots, choosing a colour-by variable (sex or CV risk), and toggling a trend line. A descriptive summary of selected variables is displayed alongside the chart.

library(shiny)
library(ggplot2)

ui <- fluidPage(
  titlePanel("Diabetes dataset explorer"),

  sidebarLayout(
    sidebarPanel(
      h4("Select variables:"),

      selectInput("var_x", "X axis:",
                  choices  = c("age", "chol", "stab.glu", "hdl",
                               "ratio", "glyhb", "weight", "waist", "hip"),
                  selected = "age"),

      selectInput("var_y", "Y axis:",
                  choices  = c("age", "chol", "stab.glu", "hdl",
                               "ratio", "glyhb", "weight", "waist", "hip"),
                  selected = "ratio"),

      selectInput("color_by", "Colour by:",
                  choices  = c("None"       = "none",
                               "Sex"        = "gender",
                               "CV risk"    = "cv_risk"),
                  selected = "gender"),

      checkboxInput("trend_line", "Show trend line", value = TRUE),
      hr(),
      helpText("Lipid and metabolic profile data (NA-cleaned).")
    ),

    mainPanel(
      plotOutput("scatter_plot"),
      hr(),
      h5("Summary statistics for selected variables:"),
      verbatimTextOutput("summary_stats")
    )
  )
)

server <- function(input, output) {

  output$scatter_plot <- renderPlot({
    p <- ggplot(Diabetes_clean, aes_string(x = input$var_x, y = input$var_y))

    if (input$color_by != "none") {
      p <- p + aes_string(color = input$color_by) +
        geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
        labs(color = input$color_by)
    } else {
      p <- p + geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue", alpha = 0.7)
    }

    if (input$trend_line) {
      p <- p + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkred")
    }

    p + theme_minimal() +
      labs(title = paste(input$var_y, "vs", input$var_x),
           x     = input$var_x,
           y     = input$var_y)
  })

  output$summary_stats <- renderPrint({
    vars <- c(input$var_x, input$var_y)
    if (input$color_by != "none") vars <- c(vars, input$color_by)
    subset_data <- Diabetes_clean[, vars, drop = FALSE]

    cat("STATISTICAL SUMMARY\n")
    if (input$color_by != "none") {
      cat("Categorical variable:", input$color_by, "\n")
      print(table(subset_data[[input$color_by]]))
      cat("\n")
    }
    cat("Numeric variables:\n")
    print(summary(subset_data[, sapply(subset_data, is.numeric), drop = FALSE]))
  })
}

shinyApp(ui = ui, server = server)

8 7 — Conclusions

The analysis confirms that machine learning models can serve as useful complementary tools for diabetes risk stratification. Both logistic regression and Random Forest achieve strong discrimination (AUC > 0.87), with the latter showing higher sensitivity (82.4%) for detecting positive cases.

Key predictors, ranked by Random Forest importance: blood glucose (stab.glu, 24.3%), age (18.7%), BMI (15.2%), HDL (12.8%), and waist-to-hip ratio (9.5%). This hierarchy validates the routine measurements already available in pharmacy consultations and highlights the lipid profile as a secondary but meaningful signal.

Limitations include the absence of lifestyle variables (diet, physical activity, family history), a moderate sample size, and substantial missing data in blood pressure variables. Future work should incorporate multiple imputation, cross-validation, and external validation on independent cohorts to improve generalisability.

Model Accuracy Sensitivity Specificity AUC
Logistic Regression 84.3% 76.5% 86.2% 0.870
Random Forest 86.0% 82.4% 87.1% 0.890
